videos.title: How to Spend 3 Days in OSLO Norway | Travel Itinerary
videos.description:
Get ready to fall in love with Oslo, Norway! We’re super excited to share our ultimate guide to spending three amazing days in this beautiful city. From its stunning landscapes to its rich culture, we’ve crafted an itinerary that will make your trip unforgettable. We’ve got your days all planned out with must-see spots and fun activities. As your travel pals, we’ll give you insider tips to help you discover Oslo’s hidden gems and local secrets that most tourists miss. Calling all food lovers! We’re taking you on a tasty tour of Oslo’s vibrant food scene. Join us as we hop from charming cafes to lively markets packed with fresh treats. We’ll introduce you to delicious local dishes that you won’t want to miss. Our dining guide covers everything—from fancy restaurants to street food delights—so you can truly savor the flavors of Oslo.

And there’s more! We’re here to help you make the most of your trip with practical advice, like the best times to visit popular spots to dodge the crowds. This way, you can enjoy a smooth and stress-free adventure.

Whether it’s your first visit or you’re coming back for more, our guide will make your three days in Oslo absolutely awesome. Let’s start this amazing journey together!

rose gardens meticulously designed flower beds expansive green Lawns and elegant fountains here you can enjoy a leisurely stroll or simply relax on the grass soaking in the peaceful surroundings entry to frogner park is free making it an ideal spot for relaxing morning inside frog neac lies the veegaland Sculpture Park an impressive open air Gallery featuring over 200 sculptures by Gustav vigeland these captivating artworks in bronze granite and cast iron make this the largest Sculpture Park by a single artist in the world the park itself is a testament to creativity and a central piece to oslo's cultural heritage lunch at Cafe veegaland located within frogner Park Cafe vialand offers a delightful spot for lunch opt for the Norwegian smoked salmon sandwich paired with a side of Fresh Garden salad costing around 15 USD this meal not only satisfies your hunger but also keeps you energized for the afternoon's Adventures afternoon island hopping in the Oslo Fjord after lunch take a short ferry ride from AA Brigade to havd doya Island just 17 minutes away the fery ticket costs about 7 USD round trip havya is the perfect escape from city Life featuring Lush forests sandy beaches and historical sites you can explore the ruins of a sersan monastery dating back to 1147 or visit the old military Cannon battery from 1808 the islands beaches and Inland hiking trails offer a videos.transcript: Hi there I'm Pål from Norway. And apart from walking the best way to get around in Oslo is by public transport. In this episode I'm going to explain to you how the public transport system works in Oslo And towards the end I'm also going to show you how you can do a public transport Fjord cruise for only 39 kroner Oslo is tied together by an extensive public transportation system consisting of Metro lines trams, buses, ferries and trains, covering most of the urban area of the city. They run from early morning until late night And it's a convenient safe fast and fairly affordable way to get around. From zipping through the downtown area to reaching the far ends of the city When you visit Oslo, public transport is your best bet for getting around Before getting on any kind of public transport you need to have a ticket. In Oslo it's super easy because there is one app to rule them all, and it's called Ruter. You can use the Ruter app for buying a ticket for all public transportation. Oslo is Zone 1, but you can also use it for traveling out of Oslo to the connecting regions. Such as for instance the Oslo Airport, which is in zone 4. You can choose between a single ticket valid for one hour, 24 hours, or several other options. The Ruter app is also great for route planning. Just enter where you're heading to. Press get directions and it will give you several options for getting there. It even shows you how long it would take to bike or walk. If you don't want to use an app you can also buy a ticket inside one of these kiosks. At Narvesen 7-Eleven. You can buy a single ticket or a 24-hour ticket. So behind me here you can see the Oslo Central Station. This is where all the trains run. However, train is not much used for traveling within the area of Oslo but more for traveling outside of Oslo. Perhaps you're going to Bergen or some other part of Norway, or perhaps you're going to the airport. Okay let's look at different public transportation options for getting around in Oslo. I just love the tram, the sound of it, the way that it Glides through the city. And the best part is that you don't need a road like here at the City Hall Square All you need are some discreet tracks in the ground. Trikk, as we say in Norwegian Because it went elecTRIC back in 1894. It carries over 50 million passengers every year in Oslo. The trick might not be the fastest option, but it's quite reliable and is often found running in narrow streets where it takes up less space than a bus. For tourists to Oslo I recommend getting on a trikk and do some sightseeing. Taking trikk 12 from downtown in either direction is a great way to see the city. Just sit back relax and watch Oslo as it glides by. If you take the number 12 west towards Majorstuen you'll ride through parts of the downtown areas, past the Akershus Fortress the city hall and the Oslo fjord on your left. You then continue on to enjoy the views of the west side of Oslo. And then get off at the Vigelandsparken, to enjoy this number one tourist attraction Heading in the other direction you can ride East to the hip and trendy neighborhood of Grünerløkka. And you'll find a ton of nice little shops, restaurants, bars and perhaps try out the best waffles in town at Haralds Vaffel. I'd recommend you to try out tram number 13 and 19 as well. They go through the barcode area, which is where I'm standing now close to the Opera House. They also take you up to the hill of Ekeberg. Where you can find the Ekeberg Sculpture Park with Contemporary Art. And some great views over Oslo. Another option is the Metro. In Oslo we have a system consisting of five lines going from the far West to the far East, running through the downtown area, and covering parts of the city center. In Norwegian we call the Metro for t-bane, t-line because of the T sitting on top of it. The t-bane shifts between running underground and overground. The t-bane table is very reliable and mostly runs on time. It covers a lot of the suburbs of Oslo, and is therefore popular for commuting. For travelers to Oslo the metro is a great way to get into the outskirts. For instance you can ride the number one to the Holmenkollen Ski Jump Or to Frognerseteren which is basically inside the forest. Or take it to Songsvann, where you find another forest area. If you're in a rush, the fastest way to zip through the downtown area is to go with the metro. You can for instance take the metro from the National Theater station and down to Jernbanetorget,the Central Station in only three minutes. Remember when you're in a metro station riding the escalators you always stand on the right hand side and then you walk on the left hand side These red city buses also run all over town it's a great way to get around in Oslo. The buses might not be as charming as the tram or as fast as the Metro But they're plentiful they run all over town and some of them even depart every five minutes Especially bus number 81 and 32 which runs through downtown are great for riding to the Barcode area where you find the Opera House and the Munch museum. Travelers who want to go to Bygdøy, the museum island, with the folk museums and maritime museums Then bus number 30 is the only way to get there with Ruter public public transport. What I also like about the buses is that most of them run on electricity so they're both silent and environmentally friendly. That was a guide to public transport in Oslo. But what about the ferries? Oslo is surrounded by Islands which are connected together with electric ferries departing from Aker Brygge, just in front of the City Hall. So these ferries have just been upgraded to fully electric. There's both indoor and outdoor seating. And the best part is that they're part of the public transport system. So this is a great way to get out on the fjord at a very low cost. You basically just buy a single ticket, depart with the ferry from the Aker Brygge in front of the City Hall. Sit on it for one hour and it drops you off at the same spot. Thanks for checking out this episode! Hit the like button subscribe to my channel and if you need a local guide when you come to Oslo make sure to look me up. Ha det bra!

videos.description:

Oslo Discovery Tour: https://www.viator.com/tours/Oslo/Oslo-Discovery-Tour-Holmenkollen-Vigeland-Park-Fram-and-the-Viking-Ship-Museum/d902-6243P7?pid=P00104625&mcid=42383&medium=link&medium_version=selector&campaign=yt-oslo-desc
---------------

Experience the captivating allure of Oslo, Norway, as we unveil the top things to do and see in this vibrant Scandinavian city. This immersive video will take you on a virtual journey through Oslo's rich history, stunning architecture, and dynamic cultural scene.

Explore the iconic Viking Ship Museum, where you can witness the remarkably preserved Viking longships that have sailed the Norwegian seas over a millennium ago. Immerse yourself in the world of Norse mythology and maritime history.

Discover the architectural masterpiece that is the Oslo Opera House. Admire its contemporary design, walk on its sloping roof for panoramic views of the city, and perhaps catch a world-class opera or ballet performance.

Indulge in the culinary delights of Oslo's dining scene at local restaurants, cafes, and food markets. Sample traditional Norwegian cuisine such as gravlaks (cured salmon) and fårikål (lamb and cabbage stew), or enjoy international flavors from around the world.

Experience the cultural diversity of Oslo's neighborhoods, from the trendy Grünerløkka district with its artistic flair to the historic charm of the Old Town, known as Gamlebyen.

Explore the fascinating history and art at the Munch Museum, dedicated to the life and works of the renowned Norwegian artist Edvard Munch. Get up close to iconic paintings like "The Scream" and learn about Munch's influential role in modern art.

Immerse yourself in nature at the Vigeland Park, the world's largest sculpture park created by a single artist. Wander through the enchanting park filled with Gustav Vigeland's striking sculptures that depict the human experience.

Experience the serenity of Oslo's fjord landscape with a boat tour. Cruise along the calm waters, take in the panoramic views of the city's shoreline, and enjoy the tranquility of the fjord.

Join us as we uncover the hidden gems and must-visit attractions of Oslo, a city where history, culture, and natural beauty come together in perfect harmony.

Get ready to be captivated by the allure of Oslo in this immersive video experience. So, pack your bags, and let the adventure begin in this remarkable city where Scandinavian charm, modernity, and warmth await!

videos.description:
Join us on an unforgettable journey through the heart of Norway with our ultimate travel guide to Oslo! 🇳🇴 In this video, we uncover the Top 20 things to do in Oslo in 2025, blending art, history, and the breathtaking natural beauty of the Oslo Fjords to help you experience the very best of Norway's capital.

This in-depth City Guide helps you plan your perfect 5-day Oslo Itinerary. Experience breathtaking fjord scenery with island-hopping adventures to Lindøya, Hovedøya, and Gressholmen on an Oslo Fjords Winter Cruise. Enjoy Oslo's many Museums such as the Munch Museum, Fram Museum, Kon-Tiki Museum, and Viking artefacts in the Historical Museum and the Norwegian Museum of Cultural History, where you can see the Stave Church from Gol, one of the last remaining in Norway (and the world). Within the city centre, explore Akershus Fortress, Oslo Opera House, Oslo City Hall, Nobel Peace Center, Ibsen Museum, Natural History Museum, and Oslo Reptile House. Next, head into the Nordmarka Forest and see Holmenkollen Ski Jump, the Bygdøy Peninsula to discover popular beaches like Huk, and much more.

videos.title: 10 BEST Things To Do In Oslo | Oslo Travel Guide
videos.description:
Oslo, the vibrant capital of Norway, offers a diverse array of activities and attractions that cater to all types of travelers.
Best Hotels In Oslo ➡️ https://booking.tp.st/9EHhs18v
DISCOUNTED Tickets for Oslo ➡️ https://getyourguide.tp.st/Qkuharo7

Whether you're visiting during the lively summer months or the serene winter season, there are countless things to do in Oslo. The city is a cultural hub, home to world-class museums like the MUNCH Museum and the National Museum, which are must-visits for art enthusiasts. For those interested in history, the Viking Ship Museum and Akershus Fortress provide fascinating insights into Norway's past.

One of the top things to do in Oslo is exploring the Vigeland Sculpture Park, which is the largest sculpture park in the world created by a single artist. It's a free attraction, making it one of the best things to do in Oslo for budget-conscious travelers. Another free activity is walking along the Akerselva River, which offers scenic views and a glimpse into Oslo's industrial heritage. During winter, Oslo transforms into a snowy wonderland. Visitors can enjoy skiing at the Holmenkollen Ski Jump or ice skating at Spikersuppa rink, making winter one of the best times to experience Oslo's outdoor activities.

For those seeking fun things to do in Oslo at night, the city offers a vibrant nightlife scene. The Grünerløkka district is known for its trendy bars and cafes, while the Oslo Opera House hosts evening performances that are a treat for culture lovers. If you're looking for cool things to do in Oslo, consider taking a fjord cruise to see the city from a different perspective or visiting the Deichman Bjørvika library, a modern architectural marvel.

Oslo is also a great destination for families, with attractions like the Norsk Folkemuseum and the Fram Museum offering interactive exhibits that are both educational and entertaining. For those who enjoy the outdoors, Oslo's proximity to nature means you can easily combine urban exploration with hiking in the surrounding forests. Whether you're interested in history, culture, or outdoor adventures, Oslo has something to offer everyone, making it a top travel destination in Norway.

videos.transcript: Hi. Hi. I'm Pål from Norway. And a very common question I get about Oslo is when is the best time to visit? Now, the high season here goes from June to August with July until mid August being the peak season with the most people around. Obviously, that's because this is vacation time in Europe. But it's also the warmest time of year and with the least chance of rain and people want to take advantage of that weather. Actually, I'm seeing more and more people coming here in July to avoid the sweltering hot summers you find further south in Europe. However, if you want almost as good weather, you can come here in May and even late August, early September, and you can experience a very pleasant climate with fairly warm and sunny days, but with much fewer people around. You can, of course, come visit all year round. Depends a bit on what your interests are. If you want to have a chance of snow on the ground, perhaps you want to ski, then come in January or February. Or if you want Christmas markets, come late November until mid December. So my personal recommendation if you enjoy traveling in the shoulder season is to come here in May, early June or in late August, September. Or for the best weather, come here in July. And if you want winter weather, you should come here January or February. To get around in Oslo, I recommend walking. It's just a great way to get to know the city. And Oslo is also very walkable. There's a lot of pedestrianized streets. Traffic is limited downtown and most of the downtown area is quite flat. Although the city is surrounded by hills. So once you're out of the downtown area, you can expect it to become more hilly. For instance, you can basically walk all the way from the east side in the neighborhood of Grünerløkka and straight through the entire city to the west side of Oslo and the Vigeland Park in just about 1 hour. So bring good shoes and explore Oslo like a local. If you want to shorten down your walk time, you can hop on a city bike. There are Oslo city bikes parked all over town. And when Rick Steves visited Oslo, I spent a full day with him and we used them to ride everywhere. These days, in major tourist destinations across Europe, such as Madrid, London, Paris, you want to book activities well ahead of time. However, for Oslo, that is not really the case. We don't get the crazy crowds of people up here, not even in summer. and things rarely book up. For instance, at the National Museum, which is the biggest museum in town, you can pretty much show up any time of the week and there won't be any long lines to get in. Same goes for perhaps the biggest attraction in Oslo, the Vigeland Park, where you don't need a ticket at all. Just enter at any time during the day. Now, there's a few exceptions here. Like, for instance, if you want to watch a performance at the Oslo Opera House, I'd advise you to book weeks or even months ahead of time. And that goes for pretty much any popular artist or cultural event taking place. Like for instance, Øya Festival, which is a big music festival in Oslo every August, you really want to book tickets well ahead of time. If you want a guided tour of the Royal Palace in summertime when it's open, I also recommend you booking ahead of time for that. There's also the Oslo Pass that gives you free public transportation in the city and access to most sites in Oslo. Or if you're heading to the Fram Museum, Kon-Tiki or Maritime Museum, know that they have a combo ticket if you want to visit more than one of them. A lot of visitors to Oslo end up leaving Oslo with the Norway in a nutshell tour, which I made a separate episode about. And this journey, I'd say it's essential to book well ahead of time, especially between May and September. It's also good to know that some tour operators will give you a discount if you book ahead of time or if you book online. So, that's also worth looking into. What I love about Oslo is its public transportation. The city is so well connected with buses, trams, metros, feries, and it's all available on one ticket and in one zone, which is zone one. You can buy tickets at a local kiosk or do as the locals download the Ruter app where you can get single tickets, 24-hour tickets, 7 days tickets. If you're only doing two to three rides a day, buying a single ticket is just as economical. You can also use the Ruter app for route planning, or if you're used to Google Maps, that works well, too. The more expensive option for getting around are taxis. There are taxi stops close to main stations, but you can also use Uber or Bolt in Oslo. Note that taxis are not cheap here, so it's more for convenience. Like I said earlier, my top recommendation is to walk around Oslo and get to know the city that way. But to save time, hop on some public transportation. You absolutely do not have to learn Norwegian in order to travel in Norway. English is widely understood and spoken. But isn't it more fun to order a kaffe and kanelbolle at a café instead of a coffee and a cinnamon bun? Most Norwegians speak English and especially in the cities, their English proficiency is excellent. However, like in any culture, Norwegians appreciate visitors who make an effort to speak the local language, which can open doors to connections and experiences you might miss otherwise. There are many ways to learn. You can use Dualingo. You can just Google some simple phrases online. However, if you're a bit more serious about it, I recommend checking out the language school Speak Norsk. I'll post a link to their website below. If you're planning a trip here soon, their downloadable guide "hvordan introdusere seg på norsk" is a nice little introduction to how to introduce yourself in Norwegian with English translations. Also available is a free ebook with essential Norwegian words and phrases. This will teach you common greetings, numbers, days of the week, common verbs, adverbs, and adjectives, and more. and it's all for free. They also offer Norwegian courses both online and in person. And in my opinion, these are some of the best courses on the market. So, it means you can start learning long before you travel to Norway. Their Viking offer is a great course package that they have designed to take you from complete beginner to advanced speaker in only 6 months. Norwegians are breakfast people, so you can expect a pretty big breakfast table at hotels. This is great for getting a good start on the day. And if you're traveling on a budget, breakfast might be your biggest meal of the day. So, you can expect bread, a lot of bread, and a lot of different kinds of spreads as well, like the iconic Norwegian brown cheese, liver pate, ham, cheese, jam, salmon, hard-boiled eggs, milk, fruit juice, pastry, and so on. We're big coffee drinkers, and you can find coffee shops all over town, and basically any kind of coffee. Even in hotels, they'll often have a good drip coffee and an espresso machine. For a nice Norwegian chain, head to Kaffebrenneriet. Or for a specialtity roster, head to Fuglen Coffee Roasters or Tim Wendelbo, which has an on-site micro roastery and high quality coffees. Lunch is typically eaten between 11:30 and 1:00. And it's not a big meal here in Norway, as it is in many other European countries. Most Norwegians do with some more bread or a salad, a sandwich, and typically we like to have coffee and some sweet pastry. There are bakeries all over town where you can find delicious Norwegian sweet buns and cakes. You could also just do a waffle or a hot dog for lunch. You find hot dog kiosks all over town. Some a bit more modern while others a bit more traditional. Dinner starts not particularly late and you can find people starting to eat already around 5:30 in restaurants, but they'll serve dinner until late. So, no worries. You really have a lot of flexibility here, but I'd say the prime time at restaurants goes from around 6:30 to 8:30 in the evening. Norway is not a tipping country, meaning that when you're in Oslo, you don't tip at cafes. And at a restaurant, you tip between 5 to 10%, but never more than that. You can usually tip when you pay with a card. Just enter a higher total sum to include the tip. In general, our attitude towards tipping is that we only tip when we're happy with the food and we're happy with the service. Most waiters here are paid fair wages, so they're not dependent on tips. But one of the things that I always say is that if you like the service, you should definitely leave something. So, at a restaurant, let's say you round up the bill or even leave 10% if you were super happy with everything. Taxis or other services might have their own rules as well, but personally, I rarely tip in a taxi. They're already quite expensive. But remember, always tip a tour guide if you're happy with the tour. Norway is becoming a cashless society, meaning that you can pay pretty much with a credit or debit card everywhere in Oslo. However, all businesses are required to accept cash. So, if that is your preference, you can use that. If you do need to withdraw cash, there are plenty of ATMs and places to exchange money all over town. Mastercard and Visa card are the most common accepted forms of payment, but you also see places accepting American Express and other types of cards. And you can also use Apple Pay on your phone. So my advice would be to bring at least two cards so that if one card is not working, the other one is. If you're confident that your cards will work, then I'd say you don't have to bring cash when you come visit Oslo. Oslo is a very safe city. Even for solo travelers, female travelers, you don't really have to worry much about crime. But like any major city in Europe, you should be aware of pickpockets. It's not a big problem here, though. I don't think us locals go around thinking much about it. But when you're out traveling, you don't want to have your vacation spoiled because you lost your passport, your cash, or your credit cards. Now, don't be paranoid. Be smart. Wear a money belt where you can keep your passport, an extra card, or so on. You can of course also leave your passport in the safety deposit box in the hotel in case you don't want to carry your passport around. So then just make a copy of it and bring that with you. There's a lot to see and do in Oslo and I would recommend spending at least two full days here. But if you have more time, you can consider doing a day trip. Now you have to choose whether you want to stay close to the fjord or if you want to venture out into the countryside. One option is to hop on a fjord ferry and ride it for about 1 hour south to the fjord village of Drøbak. This is a super cute town with white wooden houses, charming streets, small shops, and a maritime vibe. There's also a small ferry that can take you to and from the Oscarsborg fortress where you can visit a World War II museum to learn about the battle of the Drøbak sound and you'll also get amazing views of the Oslo fjord. Another option for a day trip about 2 hours north of Oslo is the town of Lillehammer. You can easily get there on a train departing every hour from the Oslo Central Station. It's an enjoyable train ride. You get to see plenty of the countryside. And once in Lillehammer , you can visit the popular Maihaugen open air folk museum, the Lysgårdsbakkene ski jumps, and stroll around in Lillehammer downtown, checking out the cute little shops and cafes. In the afternoon, you can ride the same train back to Oslo. You can buy these tickets through the VY app or on the VY website. Norwegians are quite casual, but they do like to dress up a bit when they go out for dinners and to bars and so on. So, it can be a good idea to bring a nice pair of pants, a nice sweater or button-down shirt to go with it, or a nice dress if you are a woman. But to be honest, you can get away with most things these days. However, my recommendation would be to rather be a little bit overdressed than underdressed. About weather in general, there's only one thing to say. It's unpredictable. That means you want to come prepared for different kinds of weather types and potentially four seasons in one day. When considering what kind of clothing to bring, you might wonder if Oslo is extremely cold in the winter. Well, not really. It has a coastal climate, but there can be a lot of wind chill bringing the temperatures down. In general, I say always have something waterproof with you. Dress in layers, but also bring shorts and t-shirts for hot summer days. and bring your swimming gear in case you want to jump into the Oslo fjord. I've published a Norway packing list on my website, so go online, check it out. I'll post a link below where you can learn how to dress for each season in Norway. All right, that's all the advice I had for now. If you need some ideas for things to do in Oslo, check out my two hidden gems episodes. I also work as a local guide here, so look me up if you come visit. I can also help you plan your next adventure in Norway. If you enjoy the episode, consider supporting me on Patreon. And until next time, Ha det bra!
